nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi; found in and among boulder piles and rock rubble along the shoreline, rarely venturing over open sandy substrate
nativeRange.biotope: Rocky littoral — Lake Malawi mbuna zone

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rock piles creating multiple caves and territories
minimal or no planting (cichlids will uproot most plants)
open sand substrate (coarse sand or fine gravel acceptable)
multiple visual barriers to break sightlines between territorial males
aragonite or crushed coral substrate or decor to buffer pH and hardness

diet.notes: Primarily herbivorous — algae (aufwuchs), spirulina-based flake and pellets, blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini), and algae wafers form the dietary core. A modest protein component (small frozen foods such as daphnia or mysis, Cyclops) is accepted and beneficial; avoid high-protein foods like bloodworms, which can trigger Malawi Bloat (a potentially fatal digestive condition linked to excess animal protein in mbuna). Feed 2–3 small meals daily; spirulina-enriched staple recommended.

goodWith: other robust Lake Malawi mbuna of similar size (Pseudotropheus, Labidochromis, Iodotropheus)
Lake Malawi haplochromines of significantly different coloration
Synodontis catfish (Lake Tanganyika species tolerate Malawi conditions)
avoidWith: cobalt-blue mbuna (Maylandia callainos fw-fish-303) — hybridizes freely
any similarly colored or patterned mbuna (triggers inter- and intraspecific aggression)
peaceful community fish (tetras, danios, corydoras, livebearers)
small or slow fish that cannot escape harassment
mbuna from other genera that share similar blue coloration (confusion spawning risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Maylandia zebra. Shows typical male OB or blue coloration.
Wikimedia image labeled Pseudotropheus zebra (synonym); male blue morph in aquarium.

summary: The original "Zebra" mbuna and type species of Maylandia, endemic to Lake Malawi’s rocky shores. Highly aggressive and territorial with many wild locality color forms. A maternal mouthbrooder best kept in a species-dominated mbuna community with a large, heavily decorated tank.
careNotes:
Maylandia zebra is the type species of its genus and one of the most studied African rift-lake cichlids, collected throughout Lake Malawi’s rocky littoral zone. Wild populations exhibit striking locality-based color polymorphism — the classic blue-and-black barred “Zebra” morph, the uniformly blue “Cobalt Blue” form, the orange-red “Red Top” form, and the mottled orange-and-black “Marmalade Cat” or OB (orange-blotched) pattern are all recognized as the same species. These forms are often marketed under distinct trade names, and aquarists should be aware that mixing them will produce hybrid offspring indistinguishable from pure locality fish.

A minimum of 200 L (roughly 120 cm long) is recommended for a starter group; larger tanks of 300 L or more are strongly preferred when housing multiple males or a mixed-mbuna community. Mbuna are highly territorial: keep at least one male with 3–4 females, or overstock with a large mixed mbuna community to diffuse aggression. A single male in a small tank will relentlessly bully females. Rocks must be piled to ceiling-height with interlocking caves to create dozens of territories.

Water chemistry is critical: maintain pH 7.8–8.6 and moderate hardness (gH 10–20, kH 6–15). Aragonite or crushed coral substrate is the simplest buffer. Temperature 23–28°C suits all Malawi mbuna. Weekly water changes of 30–50% are essential; nitrate accumulation above 20–25 ppm is associated with Malawi Bloat.

Diet must remain predominantly plant-based. High-protein foods (especially bloodworms, tubifex) can cause Malawi Bloat — a serious and often fatal condition in herbivorous mbuna. Spirulina flake, mbuna-specific pellets, and occasional frozen daphnia or mysis are appropriate.

Avoid keeping with Maylandia callainos (Cobalt Blue, fw-fish-303) or M. estherae (Red Zebra, fw-fish-466) unless intentional hybridization is acceptable — these three species hybridize freely in captivity and the offspring are fertile.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning is typical mbuna style: the male selects and defends a flat rock surface, performs circling and fin-display courtship, and the female deposits eggs which she immediately picks up in her buccal cavity. Fertilization occurs via the male’s egg-spot-mimicking anal fin markings (egg-dummy pattern), which triggers the female to attempt to pick up the false eggs and ingest milt. The female broods 15–40 eggs for 21–28 days at 25–26°C, releasing free-swimming fry once they have absorbed the yolk sac. Females may be stripped at day 18–21 for artificial incubation if predation risk is high. Brood sizes typically range from 15 to 40 fry. Feed released fry on finely crushed spirulina flake or micro-pellets; fry grow quickly and begin displaying adult coloration within 8–12 weeks. The female should be isolated to recover condition after brooding, as males resume courtship pressure immediately.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Maylandia zebra
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided scientific taxonomy, native range (Lake Malawi rocky shoreline), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.8, temp 22–28°C, hardness 10–20 dH), adult size (males to 12–14 cm), tank size recommendation, diet notes (herbivore/aufwuchs), breeding method (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day hold), conspecific aggression and hybridization risks with M. callainos and M. estherae.

additional.name: FishBase — Maylandia zebra
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (males to 16 cm TL in wild, aquarium specimens typically 10–14 cm), native range (Lake Malawi endemic, Malawi/Mozambique/Tanzania), depth range (1–15 m), diet (herbivore, algae/aufwuchs), and habitat (rocky littoral). Also confirmed type-species status for Maylandia. Size range consolidated to 10–14 cm aquarium typical.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mbuna Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed minimum tank size (~75 gal / 280 L for species tank), alkaline hard water requirements, spirulina-heavy diet, Malawi Bloat risk with protein-heavy feeding, and overstocking strategy for aggression management. Temperature range 75–82°F (24–28°C) consistent with other sources.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Cichlids: Malawi Mbuna
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed locality-form polymorphism (blue, cobalt, OB, red-top morphs all M. zebra), hybridization behavior and caution, breeding behavior detail (egg-dummy anal fin markings), and recommended sex ratio (1 male: 3–4 females). Also confirmed Pseudotropheus zebra as a prior synonym.

additional.name: Stauffer et al. 1997 / Stauffer & Konings 2006 — Maylandia type species designation
additional.notes: Taxonomic reference establishing M. zebra as the type species of Maylandia. The genus Metriaclima (Stauffer 1997) is treated as a synonym of Maylandia (Meyer & Foerster 1984) by many authorities including FishBase and SeriouslyFish; some publications still use Metriaclima. Both names appear in hobbyist literature.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 15–40 eggs buccally for 21–28 days. Spawning occurs readily in well-maintained tanks with appropriate rock decor. Main challenges: male aggression toward brooding female and fry predation by tankmates. Stripping females at day 18–21 improves fry yield. Fry accept micro-crushed spirulina flake from first feeding.
